content.text: Trace Retention and Audit Trail Requirements

Conversation traces for Claude carry sensitive operational metadata — session identifiers, latency breakdowns, retry counts, routing decisions — that must be preserved for incident response, performance analysis, and compliance audits, then destroyed on schedule to honour GDPR minimisation obligations. The right design defines explicit retention tiers, enforces transitions automatically, and produces an immutable audit trail that proves every access and deletion event.
This document specifies retention windows, storage tiers, audit-event schemas, and the configuration code needed to operate them in production.

attrs.alt: Trace Retention Lifecycle and Audit Trail

content.text: Why Trace Retention Requires Its Own Policy

Log and metric retention policies are simpler because those data types are aggregated — losing a single log line rarely matters. A trace, by contrast, records the complete causal chain of one request: every span, every parent-child link, every propagated context header. Two consequences follow:
A ten-hop Claude request can produce 200+ spans. Retaining all traces at full fidelity costs 10–50× more than equivalent log volume.
are not PII themselves, but they link back to a user's session record. That link triggers GDPR Article 4(1) — the data becomes personal when combined with the session store.
Audit trails must cover access, not just existence.
Compliance auditors ask who queried which trace, not just how long the trace was stored.
Define four tiers. Every trace starts in HOT and moves forward; no backward promotion is permitted except for legal holds, which freeze a trace in place until released.
After 7 yr (or sooner per GDPR erasure)

A legal hold suspends tier transitions. A trace under hold stays in its current tier indefinitely and cannot be deleted even when the clock expires. The hold is recorded in the audit trail and must be released by an authorised officer before deletion resumes.
Configuring Grafana Tempo for HOT-Tier Retention
Tempo's compactor block sets a per-tenant maximum trace age. Blocks older than the limit are deleted from object storage during the next compaction cycle.

# tempo.yaml  (compactor section)
compactor:
  compaction:
    block_retention: 720h   # 30 days = HOT tier window

# Per-tenant override (overrides.yaml)
overrides:
  "tenant-production":
    block_retention: 720h
  "tenant-staging":
    block_retention: 168h   # 7 days — staging needs less
When Tempo deletes a block it does
emit an audit event by default. Add a deletion-event hook using the Tempo compactor's
callback or subscribe to S3 object-lifecycle notifications from Tempo's backend bucket.

After day 30, a daily export job reads Tempo's backend bucket, converts OTLP blocks to Parquet with PII attributes stripped, and writes them to a WARM bucket. Athena can then query WARM traces without restoring them.

import boto3, json, pyarrow as pa, pyarrow.parquet as pq
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

WARM_BUCKET = "trace-archive-warm"
HOT_BUCKET  = "tempo-blocks-production"

STRIP_ATTRS = {
    "claude.prompt_text", "claude.response_text",
    "http.request.body", "http.response.body",
    "user.email", "user.name",
}

def strip_pii(attrs: dict) -> dict:
    return {k: v for k, v in attrs.items() if k not in STRIP_ATTRS}

def export_day_to_warm(export_date: datetime) -> int:
    """Return number of spans exported."""
    s3 = boto3.client("s3")
    prefix = f"blocks/{export_date.strftime('%Y/%m/%d')}/"
    paginator = s3.get_paginator("list_objects_v2")
    spans_written = 0
    rows = []
    for page in paginator.paginate(Bucket=HOT_BUCKET, Prefix=prefix):
        for obj in page.get("Contents", []):
            body = s3.get_object(Bucket=HOT_BUCKET, Key=obj["Key"])["Body"].read()
            spans = json.loads(body)  # simplified; real blocks are OTLP protobuf
            for span in spans:
                span["attributes"] = strip_pii(span.get("attributes", {}))
                rows.append(span)
                spans_written += 1
    if rows:
        table = pa.Table.from_pylist(rows)
        dest_key = f"year={export_date.year}/month={export_date.month:02d}/day={export_date.day:02d}/traces.parquet"
        local = Path(f"/tmp/traces_{export_date.date()}.parquet")
        pq.write_table(table, local, compression="snappy")
        s3.upload_file(str(local), WARM_BUCKET, dest_key)
        local.unlink()
        emit_audit_event("tier_transition", "HOT", "WARM", export_date, spans_written)
    return spans_written
On day 91, an S3 lifecycle rule transitions objects from WARM to Glacier Deep Archive automatically. No application code is needed — configure the rule in Terraform:

Object Lock in COMPLIANCE mode prevents even the bucket owner from deleting objects before the retention deadline. Use GOVERNANCE mode only if you need emergency override capability and have it restricted to a break-glass role in IAM.

Every event that touches a trace — access, query, tier transition, legal-hold change, deletion — must be recorded in an append-only WORM store. The audit trail is the proof of due process for regulators.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Literal
import hashlib, json, os

AuditAction = Literal[
    "trace_queried", "trace_exported", "tier_transition",
    "legal_hold_placed", "legal_hold_released",
    "deletion_attempted", "deletion_completed", "deletion_blocked",
]

@dataclass
class TraceAuditEvent:
    trace_id:       str
    action:         AuditAction
    actor:          str          # service-account or user email (hashed for PII)
    actor_role:     str
    timestamp:      str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())
    source_tier:    str = ""
    dest_tier:      str = ""
    span_count:     int = 0
    reason:         str = ""
    prev_hash:      str = ""     # SHA-256 of previous event — chain-of-custody

    def to_json(self) -> str:
        return json.dumps(asdict(self), separators=(",", ":"))

    def compute_hash(self) -> str:
        return hashlib.sha256(self.to_json().encode()).hexdigest()

# HMAC-hash actor field before writing (avoids storing email in audit log)
_AUDIT_SECRET = os.environ["AUDIT_HASH_SECRET"].encode()

def hash_actor(actor: str) -> str:
    import hmac
    return hmac.new(_AUDIT_SECRET, actor.encode(), "sha256").hexdigest()[:16]
Writing Audit Events to S3 Object Lock

import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timezone

AUDIT_BUCKET = "trace-audit-worm"

_prev_hash = ""   # module-level chain; persist to DB in production

def emit_audit_event(
    action: str,
    trace_id: str,
    actor: str,
    actor_role: str,
    **kwargs,
) -> None:
    global _prev_hash
    event = TraceAuditEvent(
        trace_id=trace_id,
        action=action,
        actor=hash_actor(actor),
        actor_role=actor_role,
        prev_hash=_prev_hash,
        **kwargs,
    )
    payload = event.to_json().encode()
    _prev_hash = event.compute_hash()

    ts = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    key = (
        f"year={ts.year}/month={ts.month:02d}/day={ts.day:02d}/"
        f"{trace_id[:8]}_{action}_{int(ts.timestamp())}.json"
    )
    boto3.client("s3").put_object(
        Bucket=AUDIT_BUCKET,
        Key=key,
        Body=payload,
        ContentType="application/json",
        # Object Lock set on the bucket; no per-object override needed
    )
Before any deletion job runs — whether triggered by lifecycle policy, GDPR erasure request, or manual sweep — it must confirm the trace is not under a legal hold.

import boto3, json
from datetime import datetime, timezone

HOLD_TABLE = "legal-holds"   # DynamoDB table

def is_on_hold(trace_id: str) -> bool:
    """Return True if this trace_id has an active legal hold."""
    ddb = boto3.resource("dynamodb")
    table = ddb.Table(HOLD_TABLE)
    result = table.get_item(Key={"trace_id": trace_id})
    item = result.get("Item")
    if not item:
        return False
    if item.get("hold_released_at"):
        return False   # hold was released
    return True

def safe_delete_trace(trace_id: str, actor: str) -> bool:
    """Delete a trace only when no hold is active. Returns True on success."""
    if is_on_hold(trace_id):
        emit_audit_event(
            "deletion_blocked", trace_id, actor, "deletion-job",
            reason="active legal hold",
        )
        return False
    # proceed with deletion from Glacier / Parquet
    emit_audit_event("deletion_completed", trace_id, actor, "deletion-job")
    return True
The WORM audit bucket is partitioned by date. Athena can query the full seven-year trail without restoring objects from Glacier — audit events stay in S3 Standard regardless of trace tier.

-- Who queried trace abc123 in the last 30 days?
SELECT
    timestamp,
    actor,
    actor_role,
    action,
    reason
FROM trace_audit
WHERE
    trace_id = 'abc123'
    AND year >= 2025
    AND month >= 6
ORDER BY timestamp DESC;

-- All deletions blocked by legal hold this month
SELECT
    timestamp,
    trace_id,
    actor
FROM trace_audit
WHERE
    action = 'deletion_blocked'
    AND year = 2025
    AND month = 7;
Restrict access to data based on need
RBAC tiers; WARM/COLD restricted to SRE+security
Deletion job checks hold, then deletes and certifies
Every access event written to WORM audit trail
S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode enforces minimum
Hash-chained audit events; chain validated nightly
A pre-merge check confirms that every sub-subtopic under conversation-trace-governance has a compliant retention configuration in its

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""validate-trace-retention.py — run in CI."""
import json, sys
from pathlib import Path

REQUIRED_MAX_DAYS = 2557   # 7 years
REQUIRED_MIN_HOT  = 29

errors = []

# Check Tempo config
for tempo_cfg in Path("config").rglob("tempo.yaml"):
    import yaml  # type: ignore
    cfg = yaml.safe_load(tempo_cfg.read_text())
    retention_h = int(cfg.get("compactor", {})
                        .get("compaction", {})
                        .get("block_retention", "0")
                        .rstrip("h") or 0)
    if retention_h < REQUIRED_MIN_HOT * 24:
        errors.append(f"{tempo_cfg}: block_retention {retention_h}h < {REQUIRED_MIN_HOT*24}h minimum")

# Check S3 lifecycle Terraform
for tf_file in Path("terraform").rglob("*.tf"):
    text = tf_file.read_text()
    if "DEEP_ARCHIVE" in text and "expiration" in text:
        # crude check — in prod use a proper HCL parser
        if str(REQUIRED_MAX_DAYS) not in text:
            errors.append(f"{tf_file}: expiration days does not match {REQUIRED_MAX_DAYS}")

if errors:
    for e in errors:
        print("FAIL:", e, file=sys.stderr)
    sys.exit(1)

print(f"OK: trace retention config validated ({REQUIRED_MAX_DAYS}d max, {REQUIRED_MIN_HOT}d HOT min)")

Trace retention for Claude conversations requires four explicit tiers — HOT (Tempo/SSD), WARM (S3/Parquet), COLD (Glacier), DELETED — with automatic transitions enforced by S3 lifecycle rules and Tempo's compactor. Every tier transition, access event, legal-hold change, and deletion must emit an immutable audit event to an S3 Object Lock bucket. Legal-hold checks guard every deletion job. Athena makes the full seven-year audit trail queryable without any operational overhead, and a CI script validates the retention configuration stays compliant across every deployment.
HOT (0–30d) stores full-fidelity spans in Tempo; configure via compactor block_retention: 720h
WARM (31–90d) stores redacted Parquet on S3 Standard; query with Athena
COLD (91d–7yr) uses Glacier Deep Archive + Object Lock COMPLIANCE to enforce the floor
Every event goes to an append-only WORM audit bucket partitioned for cheap Athena queries
Legal holds freeze tier transitions; deletion jobs must check is_on_hold() before proceeding
Hash the actor field in audit events with HMAC to avoid storing raw email addresses
